items.title: TSMC vs. Nvidia: Which AI Supercycle Growth Stock Is the Better Long-Term Buy?
items.description: The article compares Nvidia and TSMC as AI infrastructure leaders. While Nvidia dominates GPU manufacturing with 90% market share and strong forward-thinking strategy, the author argues TSMC is better positioned for long-term outperformance. As customers develop custom AI chips and diversify suppliers, Nvidia's market share will naturally erode, while TSMC benefits from being the essential manufacturing partner for all advanced chip designers. TSMC is also positioned to capitalize on data center CPU and autonomous driving trends.
items.author: Geoffrey Seiler

insights.reasoning: Recognized as dominant AI leader with 90% GPU market share, strong forward-thinking strategy, successful acquisitions (Groq, SchedMD), and continued ability to evolve. However, sentiment is tempered by acknowledgment of inevitable market share erosion as competitors emerge.

Positioned as the essential 'arms dealer' in AI chip manufacturing with near-monopoly status, technological expertise, and scale advantages. Benefits from market fragmentation as customers design custom chips. Poised to gain from data center CPU demand and autonomous driving trends, with better long-term growth potential than Nvidia.
Mentioned as an alternative GPU supplier that customers are turning to as they seek cheaper alternatives to Nvidia, but not analyzed in depth as a primary investment option in this comparison.

items.title: 2 AI Stocks That Survived the March Sell-Off -- and Look Stronger Because of It
items.description: Arm Holdings and Advanced Micro Devices both saw their stocks rise in March despite the tech sell-off. As agentic AI emerges as the next major evolution in technology, demand for high-performance CPUs is expected to surge, positioning both companies to benefit from a projected $100 billion data center CPU market by 2031. Arm is entering the physical chip market with its new CPU designs, while AMD is leveraging its established leadership in data center CPUs with its new Venice architecture.
items.author: Geoffrey Seiler

insights.reasoning: Arm is entering the physical chip market with proven CPU technology designed for agentic AI. The company targets $15 billion in revenue from new CPU chips and sees significant opportunity in the projected $100 billion data center CPU market by 2031. Its power efficiency and high core counts align well with agentic AI requirements.

AMD is the established leader in data center CPUs with $16.6 billion in data center revenue. Its new Venice architecture with improved chiplet design positions it well for agentic AI growth. The company also has two large GPU partnerships worth over $100 billion each, providing multiple growth avenues in the expanding AI infrastructure market.

Intel is mentioned as a rival to AMD in the data center CPU market where AMD has consistently gained share. No specific commentary is provided about Intel's positioning for agentic AI or future prospects.

Nvidia is mentioned as the current dominant GPU provider for AI training and inference. While the article suggests CPUs will become increasingly important for agentic AI, it does not directly criticize or praise Nvidia's future prospects.

items.title: AMD vs. Nvidia: The AI Supercycle Is Big Enough for Both. Here's the Better Buy.
items.description: While both Nvidia and AMD are positioned to benefit from the AI infrastructure boom, the article argues AMD is the better buy despite Nvidia's current dominance. Nvidia leads in AI model training with 90% GPU market share and a $4.3T market cap, but AMD offers greater upside potential through its leadership in data center CPUs, strategic deals with OpenAI and Meta for GPU inference, and positioning in the emerging agentic AI market where CPU demand is expected to surge.
items.author: Geoffrey Seiler

insights.reasoning: Recognized as the dominant leader in AI infrastructure with 90% GPU market share, $4.3T market cap, and strong positioning for next-phase AI evolution through LPU technology. However, valuation already reflects this dominance.

Positioned as the better buy with significant upside potential. Leads in data center CPUs, secured major GPU deals with OpenAI and Meta, and is well-positioned for the emerging agentic AI market where CPU demand is expected to explode.
Mentioned as one of the largest AI infrastructure spenders with a major GPU deal with AMD, indicating significant AI investment but no direct investment thesis presented.

items.title: Why This Stock Could Outperform Nvidia Over the Next 5 Years
items.description: Motley Fool contributor Jason Hall argues that Oracle could outperform Nvidia over the next five years as AI infrastructure competition intensifies. Hall suggests that companies like Broadcom and AMD may capture increasing market share in the AI chip sector, creating multiple winners in the space rather than Nvidia maintaining dominance.
items.author: Jason Hall

insights.reasoning: Positioned as a potential outperformer relative to Nvidia over the next 5 years due to AI infrastructure opportunities and competitive dynamics in the market.
Positioned as a potential outperformer relative to Nvidia over the next 5 years due to AI infrastructure opportunities and competitive dynamics in the market.
While currently dominant in AI chips, the article suggests market share erosion is likely as competitors gain ground, implying slower future growth relative to alternatives.
Expected to capture increasing market share in AI chip infrastructure, positioning it as a beneficiary of the expanding AI capex boom.
Mentioned as a competitor gaining market share in the AI chip sector, suggesting growth opportunities in the expanding artificial intelligence infrastructure market.

items.title: Stock Market Today, April 2: Intel Rises on $14.2 Billion Fab 34 Stake Repurchase
items.description: Intel stock rose 4.85% after agreeing to repurchase Apollo's 49% stake in its Fab 34 facility for $14.2 billion, restoring full ownership of the Ireland-based manufacturing plant. The move strengthens Intel's control over a key asset tied to its foundry strategy and AI/data center demand. Investors are watching upcoming quarterly results for evidence of improved profitability and margins.
items.author: Eric Trie

insights.reasoning: Stock rose 4.85% on the $14.2B Fab 34 repurchase, which restores full ownership and strengthens control over manufacturing capacity for AI/data center demand. Analysts reiterated support for the turnaround strategy, though execution risk remains around margins and capital returns.
AMD closed up 3.36% alongside Intel's gains, reflecting continued enthusiasm around chip demand in the semiconductor sector.
Nvidia ended up 0.87%, benefiting from continued industry enthusiasm around AI and data center chip demand.

items.title: 2 Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stocks I'd Buy With $1,000 Before They Rebound From the Tech Sell-Off
items.description: The article recommends buying AMD and Apple during the current tech sell-off, despite AI stocks being under pressure from geopolitical and macroeconomic challenges. AMD is positioned for strong earnings growth driven by its data center business and AI chip adoption, while Apple has significant monetization opportunities through AI-enabled software features leveraging its massive 2.5 billion device installed base.
items.author: Harsh Chauhan

insights.reasoning: Strong 34% revenue growth in 2025, robust data center business growth of 32%, widespread adoption of Instinct processors among top AI companies, and management guidance suggesting $20+ EPS potential over 3-5 years make it an attractive buy despite current 6% YTD decline.

Despite being behind competitors in generative AI, Apple maintains strong smartphone market leadership with 24.2% market share and 247.8M annual iPhone shipments. Significant untapped AI monetization opportunity through software subscriptions leveraging its 2.5 billion active device base presents long-term growth potential.
Mentioned as a company charging for advanced AI features, used as a comparison point for Apple's potential monetization strategy, but not recommended as a buy in this article.
Referenced as a hyperscaler using AMD's Epyc processors and as a competitor to Apple in generative AI smartphones, but not the focus of investment recommendation.

Mentioned as a hyperscaler launching cloud instances with AMD's Epyc CPUs, but not recommended as an investment in this article.

items.title: Oracle Stock Hasn't Been This Cheap in 3 Years -- But Is It a Buy Right Now?
items.description: Oracle's stock has plummeted 57% since September despite building powerful AI data centers with record $553 billion in order backlog. However, investors worry about the company's $124 billion debt load and heavy reliance on OpenAI, which has questionable ability to fulfill its $300 billion commitment. While Oracle's P/E ratio of 25.1 is the cheapest in 3 years, the analyst recommends waiting for market stabilization before buying.
items.author: Anthony Di Pizio

insights.reasoning: Despite strong AI infrastructure fundamentals and attractive valuation (P/E 25.1), the stock faces significant headwinds from $124 billion debt burden, heavy dependence on OpenAI's ability to pay ($300B of $553B backlog), and broader market uncertainty. The 57% stock decline and analyst recommendation to wait on sidelines outweigh positive factors.
